course_info.title: Software Engineering
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Software Engineering Principles and Process Models
learning_objectives: Understand why software engineering discipline is necessary for large systems
Compare process models: Waterfall, Agile, Scrum, Kanban
Identify the phases of the software development lifecycle

lecture_notes.introduction: Writing code is the easy part. Software engineering is about building systems that work, can be maintained, and can be extended by teams over years. This course teaches the practices that make large-scale software development possible.
key_concepts: Software crisis: why ad-hoc development fails at scale
Waterfall: sequential phases, works for well-understood requirements
Agile: iterative delivery, responding to change, customer collaboration
Scrum: sprints, roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, team), ceremonies
Technical debt: the compounding cost of shortcuts
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a real-world project failure caused by poor requirements management, then contrast with an Agile success story showing iterative delivery and customer feedback
setup_requirements: Project management tool (Jira, Linear, or GitHub Projects) access

review.value: Every shortcut taken today costs more to fix tomorrow; like financial debt, it compounds over time as new code is built on top of the shortcut, making future changes increasingly expensive
Waterfall assumes requirements are known upfront; Agile assumes they will change. For most software projects, requirements change, making Agile the better default

learning_objectives: Elicit and document software requirements using user stories and acceptance criteria
Distinguish between functional and non-functional requirements
Prioritize requirements using MoSCoW or value-effort matrices

lecture_notes.introduction: The most expensive bugs are requirements bugs — building the wrong thing. Requirements engineering is the process of understanding what the customer actually needs, not just what they say they want.
key_concepts: User stories: As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]
Acceptance criteria: Given/When/Then format for testable requirements
Functional requirements: what the system does
Non-functional requirements: performance, security, scalability, usability
MoSCoW prioritization: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a vague customer request ('build me a scheduling app') and decompose it into 15 user stories with acceptance criteria through a simulated stakeholder interview

review.value: Good user stories are Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable; stories that fail these criteria lead to unclear scope and unverifiable delivery
Given/When/Then format makes requirements testable: Given [precondition], When [action], Then [expected result]. If you cannot write acceptance criteria, the requirement is not understood

learning_objectives: Design software using established architectural patterns
Apply common design patterns: Strategy, Observer, Factory, Repository
Evaluate trade-offs between different architectural approaches
readings: Gamma et al., Design Patterns, selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Architecture is the set of decisions that are expensive to change. Choosing the right architecture up front prevents costly rewrites later. Design patterns are proven solutions to recurring problems that save you from reinventing wheels.
key_concepts: Architectural patterns: MVC, layered, microservices, event-driven
SOLID principles: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion
Design patterns: Strategy, Observer, Factory Method, Repository
Coupling and cohesion: minimizing dependencies between modules
Dependency injection: inverting control for testability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactor a tightly-coupled monolithic order processing system into a layered architecture using the Repository pattern and dependency injection, demonstrating improved testability

review.value: Good design maximizes cohesion (related code together in one module) and minimizes coupling (modules depend on each other as little as possible); this enables independent development, testing, and deployment
High-level modules should depend on abstractions (interfaces), not concrete implementations; this allows swapping implementations (e.g., test doubles) without changing the high-level code

learning_objectives: Use Git branching strategies for team collaboration
Configure continuous integration pipelines with automated testing
Understand continuous deployment and release management

lecture_notes.introduction: Version control and CI/CD are non-negotiable in professional software development. They prevent integration hell, catch bugs before they reach users, and enable teams to deliver software reliably and frequently.
key_concepts: Git branching strategies: Git Flow, GitHub Flow, trunk-based development
Pull requests: code review, approval, and merge
CI pipeline: build, lint, test, report on every push
CD pipeline: automated deployment to staging and production
Feature flags: decoupling deployment from release
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a GitHub Actions CI pipeline that runs linting, unit tests, and integration tests on every pull request, then automatically deploys to a staging environment on merge to main
setup_requirements: GitHub repository with GitHub Actions enabled
Sample project with existing test suite

review.value: All developers commit to a single main branch with short-lived feature branches (less than a day); this minimizes merge conflicts and integration risk compared to long-lived branches
Wrapping new features in configuration flags allows deploying code to production without exposing it to users; the feature is enabled separately from the deployment, reducing deployment risk

class_schedule.topic: Code Quality: Refactoring, Code Review, and Static Analysis
learning_objectives: Identify code smells and apply systematic refactoring techniques
Conduct effective code reviews that improve quality without demoralizing authors
Configure static analysis tools to catch bugs automatically
readings: Google Engineering Practices: Code Review Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Code quality is not about aesthetics — it directly affects defect rates, development speed, and team morale. Refactoring, code review, and static analysis are the three pillars of maintaining code quality over the lifetime of a project.
key_concepts: Code smells: long methods, god classes, feature envy, duplicated code
Refactoring techniques: extract method, extract class, rename, move, inline
Code review best practices: small PRs, constructive comments, focus on logic not style
Static analysis tools: linters, type checkers, security scanners
Refactoring safety: always under test coverage
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a 200-line function with multiple code smells, refactor it live into well-named functions and classes while keeping all tests passing at each step
setup_requirements: Linter and static analysis tool installed

review.value: Never refactor without tests covering the code being changed; each refactoring step should be a single change that keeps all tests green. If a test breaks, the refactoring introduced a bug
Code review effectiveness drops sharply for large pull requests; reviewers skim 500-line PRs but carefully read 50-line PRs. Split large changes into a series of small, focused PRs

learning_objectives: Design a test strategy covering unit, integration, and end-to-end testing
Write effective unit tests with mocking and dependency injection
Understand the testing pyramid and its implications for test investment
readings: Meszaros, xUnit Test Patterns, selected chapters

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing is not optional. The question is not whether to test but how much of each kind. The testing pyramid provides a framework: many fast unit tests, fewer integration tests, and even fewer slow end-to-end tests.
key_concepts: Testing pyramid: unit (base), integration (middle), E2E (top)
Unit tests: isolated, fast, testing one function or class
Integration tests: testing interaction between components
Mocking and stubbing: replacing dependencies with test doubles
Test coverage: what it measures and what it does not guarantee
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write unit tests with mocked dependencies for a service layer, then write integration tests that hit a real database, contrasting the speed and confidence trade-offs
setup_requirements: Testing framework configured (pytest, JUnit, or similar)

review.value: Many unit tests (fast, isolated, cheap), some integration tests (slower, test boundaries), few E2E tests (slow, brittle, expensive). Inverting the pyramid (many E2E, few unit) leads to slow, unreliable test suites
Stub: returns canned answers. Mock: verifies interactions. Fake: working lightweight implementation (e.g., in-memory database). Spy: records calls for later assertion

questions.question: Write three user stories with acceptance criteria for an online food ordering system. Each story should follow the 'As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]' format with at least two Given/When/Then acceptance criteria.
questions.answer:
1. As a customer, I want to search restaurants by cuisine type, so that I can find food I enjoy.
  - Given I am on the home page, When I select 'Italian' from the cuisine filter, Then only Italian restaurants are displayed.
  - Given no restaurants match my filter, When the results load, Then I see 'No restaurants found' with a suggestion to broaden my search.

2. As a customer, I want to add items to my cart from a restaurant menu, so that I can build my order.
  - Given I am viewing a restaurant menu, When I click 'Add to Cart' on an item, Then the item appears in my cart with quantity 1.
  - Given I add an item that is already in my cart, When the cart updates, Then the quantity increments by 1 rather than adding a duplicate.

3. As a restaurant owner, I want to mark items as unavailable, so that customers do not order items I cannot prepare.
  - Given I am in the menu management page, When I toggle an item to 'unavailable', Then the item appears greyed out on the customer-facing menu.
  - Given an item in a customer's cart becomes unavailable, When the customer views their cart, Then a warning is displayed and the item is removed.

rubric.key: At least two Given/When/Then per story
Stories are testable and appropriately scoped

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Agile over Waterfall for a startup building a new product? What is one scenario where Waterfall might be more appropriate?
questions.answer:
Agile's main advantage for startups is the ability to incorporate customer feedback after each iteration, reducing the risk of building the wrong product. Requirements for new products are inherently uncertain, and Agile embraces this by delivering working software frequently and adjusting direction based on real user feedback.

Waterfall may be more appropriate for safety-critical systems (medical devices, aviation software) where requirements are well-understood, regulatory documentation is mandatory, and changes after implementation are extremely costly. These domains benefit from the thorough upfront requirements and design phases of Waterfall.

rubric.key: Explains Agile advantage: feedback and adaptability

questions.question: Explain the Dependency Inversion Principle and show how it improves testability with a concrete code example.

rubric.key: Explains testability benefit with concrete example
Shows before/after code or clear pseudocode

questions.question: Describe the three stages of a CI pipeline and what each stage catches that the previous one does not.
questions.answer:
1. Lint/Format: Catches style violations, unused imports, and simple errors (undefined variables, unreachable code). Runs in seconds.
2. Unit Tests: Catches logic bugs in individual functions and classes. Runs in seconds to minutes. Catches bugs that pass linting because they are syntactically valid but semantically wrong.
3. Integration Tests: Catches interface mismatches between components, database schema issues, and API contract violations. Runs in minutes. Catches bugs where each unit works correctly in isolation but fails when connected.

rubric.key: Explains the incremental value of each stage

major_assignments.title: Team Software Development Project
major_assignments.description: Work in a team of 4 to build a full-stack web application using Agile methodology. Deliver working software in two 2-week sprints with requirements documentation, architecture design, CI/CD pipeline, comprehensive tests, and sprint retrospectives.
objectives: Practice Agile development with sprint planning, daily standups, and retrospectives
Design and implement a full-stack application with proper architecture
Set up and maintain a CI/CD pipeline with automated testing
Conduct code reviews and manage technical debt systematically
deliverables: Working web application deployed to a staging environment
Product backlog with prioritized user stories and acceptance criteria
CI/CD pipeline configuration with passing tests
Sprint retrospective reports for each sprint
Architecture decision records documenting key design choices

criteria: Sprint planning with properly estimated and prioritized stories
Daily standup evidence (meeting notes or async updates)
Sprint retrospectives with specific action items
Consistent use of project management tool
Clean architecture with clear separation of concerns
Code review evidence: every PR has at least one review
CI pipeline runs lint, unit, and integration tests on every PR
No critical bugs in the deployed application
Unit test coverage of core business logic
Integration tests for API endpoints
Tests are meaningful (would fail if code is broken)
All tests pass in CI
Git history shows meaningful commits from all team members
Peer evaluation indicates equitable contribution
Code review participation from all team members
Sprint retrospective participation
Working application delivered through disciplined Agile process
Clean architecture with CI/CD and comprehensive testing
Evidence of effective code review and quality practices
Equitable team contribution with meaningful retrospectives

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of SE concepts and practices

lab_assignments.description: Individual exercises in Git, testing, CI/CD, and refactoring

major_assignments.description: Team project with sprints and deliverables

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering requirements, architecture, and process

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all SE topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for sprint planning, reviews, and retrospectives. More than 2 unexcused absences from team ceremonies may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Sprint deliverables have firm deadlines aligned with sprint boundaries. Late individual assignments lose 10% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Team project requires collaboration. Individual assignments and exam answers must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original team work or properly attributed open-source. Using code from other teams or previous semesters constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need Git, a CI/CD platform (GitHub Actions), an IDE, and a deployment target (Heroku, Render, or similar). Lab computers are available.
